title: Berlin in 48 Hours
intro: Berlin wears its history on the outside — in bullet-pocked facades, painted slabs of fallen Wall, and the gap where a death strip used to run. This weekend trades the checklist sprint for two walkable arcs: Day 1 anchors in monumental Mitte and the courtyard-laced Scheunenviertel, Day 2 crosses the Spree into the grittier, hungrier east of Kreuzberg and Friedrichshain. Two days, eight stops, and a city still busy reinventing itself.

days.label: Day 1
days.subtitle: Mitte Landmarks, Museum Island & Old Berlin Cooking

blocks.title: The Gate That Watched It All
blocks.body: Arrive on Pariser Platz before the tour buses and the segway tours, when the sandstone columns and the bronze Quadriga overhead catch the low morning light and the square is briefly, improbably calm. This was the arch stranded in the Wall's no-man's-land for 28 years, the backdrop to the 1989 crowds who finally streamed through it. Stand under it for a moment before the day fills it up.
blocks.tip: Walk two minutes south to the Memorial to the Murdered Jews of Europe while it is still quiet — the field of stelae is at its most affecting nearly empty.

blocks.title: Coffee in a Hidden Courtyard
blocks.body: Slip off Münzstraße into a quiet back courtyard and the noise of Hackescher Markt falls away at the door of Father Carpenter, a calm, design-forward room built for a slow cortado. Order a flat white and a brunch plate, and take the table in the courtyard if the weather allows. It is the kind of pause that resets you before the marble and antiquity ahead.
blocks.tip: Cash and cards both work, but the place is genuinely small — go before the brunch surge to land a seat.

blocks.title: Five Millennia on a River Island
blocks.body: Cross to the Spree island where five neoclassical museums stand shoulder to shoulder, a UNESCO ensemble of colonnades and domes you enter through the airy James-Simon-Galerie. You cannot do all five in an afternoon, so choose one obsession — Nefertiti's painted bust in the Neues Museum, or the glazed blue brick of Babylon's Ishtar Gate — and let the rest wait for a future trip. Budget two hours and resist the urge to rush.
blocks.tip: A Museumsinsel day pass covers every house and saves money; nearly all of them close on Mondays, so plan a weekend visit accordingly.

blocks.title: Pork Knuckle in Berlin's Oldest Tavern
blocks.body: End the day where Berliners have been eating since 1621, in a cozy warren of rooms warmed by an old tiled ceramic stove. The Eisbein — a glistening braised pork knuckle — and the Sauerbraten are the dishes to order, washed down with a cold pilsner under low beams that have survived three centuries and one very bad twentieth one. Ask for the upstairs room beside the stove.
blocks.tip: It is small and popular — book a few days ahead for a weekend dinner, especially if you want the room by the oven.

days.label: Day 2
days.subtitle: The Wall, the Kebab Line & a Kreuzberg Night

blocks.title: A Mile of Painted Wall
blocks.body: Walk the 1.3-kilometer riverside stretch of the Berlin Wall that 118 artists turned into the world's longest open-air gallery in 1990, the murals still defiant against the Spree. Find Dmitri Vrubel's fraternal kiss between Brezhnev and Honecker, and Birgit Kinder's Trabant bursting through the concrete — the most photographed slabs in the city. Go early and the light and the crowds are both on your side.
blocks.tip: Come before midday for unobstructed photos; by afternoon the most famous murals are ringed three deep with selfie-takers.

blocks.title: The Most Famous Kebab Line in Germany
blocks.body: Cross into Kreuzberg for the Gemüse Kebap that launched a thousand pilgrimages — döner stuffed with grilled vegetables, crumbled feta, herbs, and a squeeze of lemon, all crackling off the flat-top grill. The line is the stuff of legend and can genuinely run long, but eaten standing on the Mehringdamm pavement it tastes like the whole multicultural city in a flatbread. This is Berlin street food at its purest.
blocks.tip: If the queue is brutal, Curry 36 is steps away for a currywurst — or come back mid-afternoon when the lunch rush thins.

blocks.title: Memory in a Building of Voids
blocks.body: A short walk north brings you to Daniel Libeskind's zinc-clad zigzag, a building that is itself an argument — slashed with diagonal 'voids' and anchored by the disorienting Garden of Exile and the cold, echoing Holocaust Tower. The permanent exhibition traces two millennia of German-Jewish life through everyday objects and searching installations. It is among the most moving museums in Europe and admission is free.
blocks.tip: Permanent-exhibition entry is free but a timed ticket is wise, and allow a few minutes for airport-style security at the door.

blocks.title: Old-Berlin Klopse, Then a Red-Lit Nightcap
blocks.body: Settle into the vaulted, old-Berlin rooms of Max und Moritz, a historic Kreuzberg brewery-tavern where the Königsberger Klopse — meatballs in a silky caper sauce — are the unmissable order, alongside Buletten and a house brew. Afterward drift toward Kottbusser Tor and the plush, red-lit calm of Würgeengel, a 1950s-styled cocktail room named for a Buñuel film, for a Negroni to close the weekend. It is the ideal soft landing into a Kreuzberg night.
blocks.tip: Reserve Max und Moritz for a weekend dinner; the back rooms fill with locals and the Klopse are worth not gambling on a table.
